title: How leaders are bridging the gap in women's health research and innovation: Kearney
gist: Kearney's global healthcare lead Paula Bellostas Muguerza argues women's health is structurally under-researched and under-funded, creating a trillion-dollar economic opportunity in gender-precision medicine, femtech and corporate benefits. She details the [W]Health platform of 350 organizations and its gender health index, and frames burnout as an under-managed organizational risk through her own hemiplegic-migraine collapse.

themes.theme: The Women's Health Gap: Scope and Root Causes

insights.claim: Only 7% of healthcare research focuses on conditions that exclusively affect women.
insights.speaker: Linda Lacina
insights.so_what: A quantified research-funding gap flags whitespace for pharma, diagnostics and femtech firms that build female-specific evidence bases before incumbents pivot.
insights.quote: Only 7% of healthcare research focuses on conditions that exclusively affect women.
insights.locator: Only 7% of healthcare research focuses

insights.claim: Only 5% of available medications have been fully monitored, tested, or labeled with the right safety information for women who are pregnant or breastfeeding.
insights.speaker: Linda Lacina
insights.so_what: Massive unlabeled-indication gap signals regulatory and product-liability risk for pharma but also a durable market for maternal-safety data and femtech testing platforms.
insights.quote: only 5% of available medications have been fully monitored, tested, or even labeled with the right safety information for women who are pregnant or breastfeeding
insights.locator: Only 7% of healthcare research focuses

insights.claim: Women live in poor health for roughly 25% more of their lives compared to men, despite living longer on average.
insights.speaker: Linda Lacina
insights.so_what: The gap between lifespan and healthspan is the addressable market: therapies and services that compress female morbidity target a demonstrably large unmet need.
insights.quote: women live in poor health for 25% more of their lives compared to men
insights.locator: Only 7% of healthcare research focuses

insights.claim: A common misconception holds that because women live longer there is no women's health gap, but the gap lies in the difference between lifespan and healthspan.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Reframing longevity as time-in-illness reorients investment toward quality-of-life outcomes rather than mortality endpoints.
insights.quote: even though we live longer we spend more time in ill health and disability
insights.locator: There is a really common misconception

insights.claim: The women's health gap stems from three causes: poor scientific understanding of female biology, chronic underinvestment in studying that biology, and persistent bias across the entire health value chain.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Naming bias as system-wide (research to diagnosis to treatment) means point solutions underperform; investors should favor platforms attacking multiple links at once.
insights.locator: There is a really common misconception

insights.claim: Women were excluded from clinical trials starting in 1993 following past fetal-development complications, leaving a structural data deficit that persists today.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Decades of missing female trial data is the root cause of the labeling gap; sex-disaggregated data generation is a foundational, defensible investment theme.
insights.quote: women weren't included in clinical trials from 1993
insights.locator: It's very multifactorial, the problem

insights.claim: The brain physiology and biology of males and females are fundamentally different, a distinction only now beginning to be understood.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Sex-specific neuroscience is an early-stage frontier; positioning ahead of consensus could capture value in CNS drug development and diagnostics.
insights.quote: the brain physiology and biology of a male and a female are completely different
insights.locator: if we expand the definition of the mental health gap

insights.claim: Women are twice as likely as men to develop depression and twice as likely to develop anxiety.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: 2x prevalence concentrates the mental-health market in women, favoring female-tailored digital-therapeutics and pharma approaches.
insights.quote: we are more prone to develop depression two times as much, the same with anxiety, two times that much
insights.locator: if we expand the definition of the mental health gap

insights.claim: Women account for roughly 70% of the prevalence of neurodegenerative disorders such as MS, Alzheimer's and dementia.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Female-skewed neurodegeneration prevalence argues that Alzheimer's and MS pipelines under-optimized for female biology are leaving efficacy on the table.
insights.quote: we actually probably represent about 70% of the prevalence of those diseases
insights.locator: if we expand the definition of the mental health gap

insights.claim: Studying the female brain is a fundamental lever for enabling both longer and healthier lives.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Ties women's brain research to the broader longevity investment narrative, expanding its addressable investor audience.
insights.quote: studying the brain of women is going to be a real fundamental analogue for us to live healthier lives and also to live longer
insights.locator: if we expand the definition of the mental health gap

themes.theme: The Economic Case for Closing the Gap

insights.claim: Closing the women's health gap could inject $1 trillion into the global economy over the next 15 years, per a World Economic Forum report.
insights.speaker: Linda Lacina
insights.so_what: A headline trillion-dollar GDP figure is the anchor thesis that pulls generalist capital into women's-health as a megatrend, not a niche.
insights.quote: They can even provide a trillion-dollar jolt to GDP in the next 15 years.
insights.locator: Only 7% of healthcare research focuses

insights.claim: Bridging the women's health gap could give each woman roughly seven more healthy days per year.
insights.speaker: Linda Lacina
insights.so_what: Converting the gap into per-capita healthy-days quantifies productivity upside relevant to employers and insurers modeling benefit ROI.
insights.quote: bridging the women's health gap can give women seven more healthy days a year
insights.locator: Only 7% of healthcare research focuses

insights.claim: Closing the women's health gap is cited as an opportunity to unlock $400 billion annually for the global economy.
insights.speaker: World Economic Forum
insights.so_what: An alternate sizing figure gives investors a second data point to triangulate the market's magnitude.
insights.quote: How to close the women's health gap and unlock $400 billion annually for the global economy
insights.locator: Have you read?

insights.claim: Women make about 80% of household economic and health decisions, effectively serving as the chief medical officers of the home.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Female purchasing control over health spend makes women the primary customer for consumer-health and femtech go-to-market, not just the patient.
insights.quote: women make 80% of the economic and the health decisions of the family, they're the chief medical officers of the home
insights.locator: It will be amazing.

insights.claim: Women represent between 70% and 80% of the healthcare workforce in the US and globally, creating a multiplier effect where their ill health degrades care for everyone.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Female health as an input to overall care-system capacity strengthens the systemic case for employer and payer investment in women's health.
insights.quote: they represent between 70 and 80% of the healthcare workforce. So if women aren't healthy, they're not gonna be able to look after other people
insights.locator: It will be amazing.

insights.claim: Health innovation should stop treating women as small men and instead develop drugs and diagnostics tailored to female biology, an approach termed gender precision medicine.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Gender precision medicine names an emerging product category; first movers in sex-specific dosing and diagnostics can build differentiated pipelines.
insights.quote: we don't think as women as small men
insights.locator: It will be amazing.

insights.claim: Care should be organized around a woman's whole life course rather than fragmented body-part appointments, spanning pre-maternal, maternal and menopause stages.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Integrated life-course care models point to platform opportunities in coordinated women's-health services versus siloed point solutions.
insights.quote: They would look at you in the round and across your life course
insights.locator: It will be amazing.

insights.claim: Once investment banks and private equity funds recognize the economic opportunity, funding will flow into women's health and accelerate innovation.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Implies capital is the current bottleneck; an inflection in institutional allocation could re-rate femtech and women's-health assets.
insights.quote: all of the investment banks and the private equity funds would see the economic opportunity
insights.locator: It will be amazing.

themes.theme: [W]Health Platform and the Gender Health Index

insights.claim: [W]Health began as an open letter co-created with FemTechnology and Oriana Craft that recruited 50 organizations and has since grown into a coalition of 350 organizations.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: A 7x-scaled multi-stakeholder coalition signals institutional momentum and a distribution channel investors and vendors can plug into.
insights.quote: we are now at 350 organizations that are working on all of the levers
insights.locator: when we started to understand the problem

insights.claim: The organization is premised on the thesis that women's health is wealth and that closing the gap requires collective, cross-organizational action rather than individual effort.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: The wealth framing reframes women's health from charity to economic strategy, easing corporate and investor buy-in.
insights.quote: we think that women's health is wealth
insights.locator: when we started to understand the problem

insights.claim: [W]Health launched an index that lets value-chain players (pharma, consumer health, medtech, investors, providers and payers) benchmark their own operations on closing the gender health gap.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: A standardized scoring tool creates measurable ESG-style benchmarks that could shape capital allocation and vendor selection across the sector.
insights.locator: So [W]Health has launched a special index

insights.claim: The index is designed for year-on-year benchmarking so organizations and the sector overall can track whether they have moved the needle.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Longitudinal scoring turns the index into a recurring data asset and potential accountability lever for stakeholders.
insights.locator: So [W]Health has launched a special index

themes.theme: Corporate Employer Benefits and the Microsoft Pilot

insights.claim: Any large employer, regardless of sector, employs women and should manage their health because healthy women drive healthy corporations and a healthy economy.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Positions women's-health benefits as a cross-sector employer-purchasing market, expanding the TAM beyond healthcare companies.
insights.quote: it's healthy women, healthy corporations, healthy economy
insights.locator: there's a flip side of the index

insights.claim: A beta test of the employer index with Microsoft found its women's-health benefits were patchy and lacked end-to-end life-course thinking across menstruation, PCOS, endometriosis, fertility and menopause.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: If a sophisticated employer like Microsoft has fragmented benefits, integrated life-course benefit platforms have a large upgrade market even among leaders.
insights.quote: it was very patchy. So it was, I'll have a fertility benefit over here, and I'll a menopause benefit over there
insights.locator: we beta tested the employer index with Microsoft

insights.claim: Large global corporations apply very different women's-health approaches across countries, leaving best practices from one market unscaled to others.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Cross-country inconsistency is a specific inefficiency that global benefits-harmonization vendors can monetize.
insights.quote: they have very different approaches in the different countries
insights.locator: we beta tested the employer index with Microsoft

insights.claim: Investing in women's health within organizations reduces absenteeism and helps women progress in their careers, since representation drives further representation.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Links benefits spend to measurable HR outcomes (absenteeism, retention), giving CFOs a productivity-based ROI case.
insights.quote: we make sure those women progress in their career
insights.locator: there's a flip side of the index

themes.theme: Burnout as an Organizational and Leadership Risk

insights.claim: Sustained overwork triggered a rare epigenetic hemiplegic migraine in the speaker, a permanent condition activated by high cortisol and adrenaline once a genetic predisposition is stressed.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: A concrete case that chronic stress can trigger irreversible physical illness underscores the tangible liability of unmanaged burnout.
insights.quote: you have it in your genes that you could have the potential to develop this, but it only manifests if you have put your body under a lot of strain
insights.locator: I'd love to tell you about my burnout story

insights.claim: Superwoman syndrome and chronic people-pleasing led the speaker to absorb a departing colleague's workload, precipitating a health collapse within about three months.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Identifies a behavioral pattern managers can screen for as an early leading indicator of burnout risk in high performers.
insights.quote: I have this sort of like superwoman syndrome of I must save the situation
insights.locator: I'd love to tell you about my burnout story

insights.claim: The speaker was passed between neurologists, cardiologists and psychologists without diagnosis and was unable to work for six months, illustrating how women's symptoms fall through system gaps.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Diagnostic odyssey exemplifies the fragmented-care failure that coordinated women's-health and diagnostic ventures aim to solve.
insights.quote: I got passed between so many different people and it was neurologists and it's cardiologists
insights.locator: I'd love to tell you about my burnout story

insights.claim: Burnout was studied and first recognized by the WHO roughly 50 years ago, originating from a German community-support team, and presents as inability to rest, a constantly whirring mind, quickness to anger, and paralysis in starting work.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: A concrete symptom checklist gives leaders an operational tool to detect burnout early in themselves and teams.
insights.quote: you can't see the wood for the trees and you almost don't know where to start
insights.locator: Let me start by talking about how to identify burnout

insights.claim: Leaders must create a culture of openness about mental health because post-pandemic latent trauma combined with a renewed push for productivity and presenteeism is eroding gains made during COVID-19.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: Flags a reversal in workplace mental-health progress that raises attrition and burnout risk for productivity-driven organizations.
insights.quote: we all have a little bit of post-traumatic stress after the pandemic that it is just bubbling in there and we haven't addressed
insights.locator: Let me start by talking about how to identify burnout

insights.claim: Recovery required shifting from extrinsic motivation (money, visibility, external validation) to intrinsic motivation, alongside learning to set boundaries and a graduated phased return to work.
insights.speaker: Paula Bellostas Muguerza
insights.so_what: The extrinsic-to-intrinsic reframe plus graduated return offers a repeatable playbook for sustainable high-performer retention.
insights.quote: if you're intrinsically motivated, you don't need as much of the external stuff
insights.locator: post-burnout, I had to kind of like reset
